deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding rather than giving answers—e.g., never says something like 'I'm asking you to work this out yourself because active reasoning builds retention and philosophical skill.' The tutoring constraints are enforced but the rationale for them is never articulated to the student.

In transcripts 1 and 2, when students make meaningful improvements (e.g., arriving at the means/side-effect distinction after earlier confusion), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in their thinking or why the new approach worked better. No turn explicitly asks the student to reflect on their own reasoning progress.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts the tutor performs strongly: it consistently avoids giving submission-ready answers, keeps key idea generation student-led, scaffolds well by building on student progress, and maintains a supportive tone. The main weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the Socratic approach (1.3.A.a) and a missed opportunity to prompt reflection after students improve (1.3.C.a). No deductions were applied for 1.1 or 1.2 as the tutor reliably uses guided questioning, acknowledges correct student steps, and redirects confusion appropriately.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gives final answers or near-submission-ready content; it consistently asks the student to generate claims, test principles, and draft outlines. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor builds on each correct student step (e.g., affirming the means/side-effect distinction before pushing further) and diagnoses confusion before responding (e.g., Transcript 1 Turn 3 isolates the 'different act' sticking point before proceeding). No deductions apply. 1.3: The tutoring rationale is never explained to the student in any of the three conversations—constraints are enforced but never justified pedagogically, triggering 1.3.A.a. Additionally, when students make clear improvements (e.g., arriving at a coherent principle after earlier confusion), the tutor advances the task without asking the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking, triggering 1.3.C.a. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetitive spiraling or off-topic drift observed. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses are appropriately concise, interactive, and supportive throughout; no grading language is used.
